data.title: The Supreme Court May Have Just Prevented A Recession
data.description:
February 20th was a really fascinating day for market participants. For months now, it had been anticipated that the US Supreme Court would eventually rule on the tariffs imposed by Donald Trump under the IEEPA (International Emergency Economic Powers Act). And finally, the results came out. The decision by the court was that the implementation of the tariffs under this act was unconstitutional. This immediately wipes the slate clean when it comes to tariff implementation. But that doesn't mean that the doors to this regressive tax policy are closed. In fact, shortly after the news broke, Donald Trump revealed new tariffs under another act. And it is likely that while these existing tariffs must be temporary, newer tariffs under other aspects of the law will be attempted.

I always do my best to keep political views outside of my investing analysis. I strip away the biases and look at the substance. And in fact, there are political views from both sides of the aisle that I support, as well as views on both sides that I disagree with. So with that in mind, I can say that I am quite critical of broad-based tariffs. Their regressive nature, combined with other government actions such as those reducing health care access and reducing government employment, risks pushing the US into a recession. In fact, starting in August of last year, I said that a recession is all but guaranteed.

When I first heard the news that the US Supreme Court had given the axe to these tariffs, I was initially elated. But the subsequent decision by the administration to implement new ones means that my overall assessment of the economy is, at least for now, unchanged. Based on all the data that I have seen, we are heading toward a recession. And I believe that investors would be wise to act accordingly.

A fascinating development

I was probably as surprised as anyone when the news scrolled across my screen saying that the US Supreme Court ruled that tariffs under the IEEPA were unconstitutional. I wasn't surprised at the decision so much as I was at the timing. I had figured it would have been announced later in the year. For context, starting last year, shortly after he came into office again, Donald Trump started imposing tariffs on various countries, invoking the IEEPA and the authority he believed it grants the President. Under the act, the President is granted the authority to 'investigate, block during the pendency of an investigation, regulate, direct and compel, nullify, void, prevent, or prohibit… importation or exportation.’

Under the current administration, the belief was that this language allowed the President to impose tariffs since they dealt directly with imports and exports. The ruling, however, points out that there is no mention of tariffs or duties in the act at all. The assertion, then, was that if Congress had intended to convey the power to impose tariffs to the Executive branch, it would have been explicit in the act in the way that it has been explicit in tariff statutes. Whether or not you agree with the imposition of tariffs, I think that this logic is sound, though I would say that I can also understand why some would push back since the word ‘regulate’ could be construed to mean 'to impose tariffs.' But at the end of the day, absent an explicit mention in another act, only Congress has the power to impose taxes on the American people or companies. And make no mistake. Tariffs are, in fact, a tax on the American public.

This is not my opinion. In fact, the Federal Reserve recently said as much. In their own study, published on February 12th of this year, they looked at the effective impact that tariffs have had on the American people. As the image above illustrates, in the first eight months of 2025, importers, which would be those that received the imports, absorbed 94% of the increase in prices caused by tariffs. It is noteworthy, however, that as the year progressed, this rate did drop. By November, it was down to 86%. This decline can almost certainly be attributable to the fact that companies became nimbler. They reorganized their supply chains and almost certainly intensified negotiations with foreign product providers. We do see in the image below, as an example, that compared to 2017 and 2024, the percentage of import value changed significantly in some respects. The US saw the share of imports coming from China drop considerably from around 24% in 2017 to about 9% last year. Meanwhile, other countries have seen exposure increase. Mexico and Vietnam are two such examples.

The big question is what happens next. If the tariff matter was going to be dropped entirely, I would become much more neutral on the economy. But there are actions being taken by the current Administration that will leave in place, at least for now, some tariffs. In a public statement that was made following the decision, Donald Trump said that he was invoking Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974. Under this section, when there are 'large and serious balance-of-payments deficits’ impacting the US, the President has the authority to impose tariffs of up to 15% for a span of no more than 150 days. That 150-day mark has been initiated, though the tariff rate has been capped at 10% globally.

For countries such as China, as well as others that were severely negatively impacted by much higher tariffs, this is a win. It's also noteworthy that unless Congress acts, the 150-day mark is a hard limit under Section 122. From my understanding, it cannot be rolled over into a subsequent 150-day window of time. For some, this might seem like the end of the tariff drama. But there are other avenues that the Administration has that it can implement in order to achieve its goals.

Under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, tariffs can be imposed by the President. However, before that occurs, the Secretary of Commerce must conduct an investigation. It must then be determined whether imports threatened to impair national security. And in the event that they do, the President has the authority to impose tariffs or quotas, or to adjust imports using other mechanisms. Seeing as how this avenue has no explicit tariff cap and can technically last indefinitely, this is a serious option that's on the table. But it will take time. On top of this, any sort of investigation and report that would conclude that sweeping tariffs are appropriate will face intense pushback from Congress and also risks being taken to the Supreme Court. It is worth noting that steel and aluminum tariffs have, in the past, been imposed under this authorization.

There's also the Trade Act of 1974. Under Section 301, the US Trade Representative can initiate an investigation. If the conclusion of that investigation is that a specific foreign country is engaging in unfair practices, then the President can impose tariffs or take other retaliatory trade-related actions. The tariffs that were imposed upon China in 2018 would be one successful example of this implementation. And then we also have Section 201 of the Trade Act of 1974. Through this section, the US International Trade Commission must initiate an investigation. This would be in response to alleged injuries from global competition or a sudden surge of imports. If it determines that a serious injury has been incurred, then tariffs can be imposed on a temporary basis. There can also be other actions, such as quotas. But this is not a short-term action necessarily. Even though it is limited in time, it's typically capped at four years, with the option to extend that longer. Solar panel tariffs imposed back in 2018 fell under this category.

The most likely of these paths, in my opinion, would be none other than Section 232. This seems to me, as somebody who is not a lawyer, to be more subjective in terms of determination. The argument could be made, though I think it would fail under intense scrutiny, that national security will be impacted in a negative way by a persistent trade deficit and, if the government sticks with its previous rhetoric, drug trafficking from certain nations. If I had to guess, I would say that before this 150-day window ends, this would be the second, and that will ultimately be imposed or at least attempted. If it fails, I would say that the chance of the other two sections working out would be very close to zero.

The economy is looking worse

The COVID-19 pandemic might seem dead and buried by now. But its effects continue to ripple through this country in a way that's not too different from how 9/11 did. At the start of the pandemic, the economy contracted. Job losses mounted, and lifestyles changed drastically. Nobody, not even myself, anticipated that we would see the economy rebound as quickly as we ultimately did once things started dying down. This caused significant supply chain issues. It didn't help that government stimulus under both the Trump and Biden administrations caused significant amounts of capital to be injected into the economy. The end result of these disruptions, combined with low-interest rates and quantitative easing, was a surge in asset prices. The median home price exploded, and inflation hit multi-decade highs.

Although these conditions were challenging, the Federal Reserve acted quickly. Certain government actions also played a role in helping to bring down inflation faster than what we saw in any other major country on the planet. But the end result was high-interest rates that have caused the housing market to struggle in recent years and plunging consumer confidence. Speaking of consumer confidence, in the chart above, you can see that, right now, it's near all-time lows. Just as it seemed as though the worst was over and that declining interest rates would be the new normal, tariffs were implemented. This created inflationary pressures that proved harmful for the American people.

According to the Tax Foundation, in 2025, tariffs imposed since the start of that year amounted to an average tax increase per US household of about $1,000. Most of this impact came from the tariffs implemented under the IEEPA. But not all of them. For instance, there were some tariffs imposed under Section 232. According to that source, the remaining new tariffs under Section 232 will still cost the average household in the US about $400 this year. That is still friction at a time when consumers cannot afford it. But at least it does mark a meaningful improvement.

These tariffs will unfortunately weaken GDP moving forward. In fact, the expectation is that it will create a permanent drag on GDP of about 0.2% per year. This does not even include impacts associated with retaliation from other nations. Yes, it will raise around $490 billion of tax revenue over the next decade. However, as we discussed already, this is money coming largely out of the pockets of American companies and consumers.

This impact alone would not be enough, in my opinion, to risk a recession if it were not for the fact that the US economy is already on shaky ground. Take jobs data as an example. As the chart above illustrates, the total number of jobs in the US is growing slower now than it was in previous years. The most recent month that we have data available for would be January of 2026. The total number of non-farm jobs amounted to 158.63 million. A year earlier, it was 158.27 million. That's an increase over the last 12 months of just 359,000 jobs. This actually matches up quite well with some other data. Originally, it was estimated that the US economy added 584,000 jobs for all of last year. But that number has been revised lower by the US Bureau of Labor Statistics to just 181,000. This is a much more important indicator, in my view, than the January jobs figure that showed robust growth of 130,000 jobs. After all, revised data has the benefit of additional inputs that went into it.

Taking the January to January data for the last several years, we can see in the table above just how many jobs have been added or lost over time. For instance, from January of 2024 through January of 2025, the US economy added 1.24 million jobs. The year before that, the addition was 2.26 million. Admittedly, there were certain disruptions, mostly starting in 2020 and extending through at least 2023. The rapid loss of jobs because of the COVID-19 pandemic, followed by a recovery, certainly created some comparability issues. But even if we look at prior years, we don't see any time under which the jobs picture has been this bad outside of the pandemic. In fact, starting in the 12 months ending in January of 2013 and extending all the way up until the pandemic, the number of jobs added was drastically higher than what we have seen lately.

It's not just the total number of jobs that is concerning. It's also the fact that job openings are worsening. The most recent data that we have for this metric is from December of 2025. During that month, we saw only 6.54 million job openings. That's down 12.9% from the 7.51 million reported a year earlier. This is the lowest that we have seen since September of 2020. And if we ignore the time of the COVID-19 pandemic, you would have to go all the way back to December of 2017 to see a month when job openings were worse than this.

Takeaway

The way I see it, we have two different paths that lie ahead. The best one would be one in which tariffs are vanquished for good. This would immediately improve the condition for the vast majority of Americans. The other path is the one that we seem to be on. And this is one in which further attempts are made at imposing tariffs for the long haul. If this path is indeed the one we are destined for, it is my stance that economic conditions will continue to be fragile. The fact that the most extreme tariffs are now off the table, at least for the moment, makes me feel a bit more optimistic about the state of the economy. But until we see data improve drastically, or until we see a change in overall policy, I still maintain that a recession is a high-probability event right now.

data.title: Park Hotels & Resorts: World Cup, Renovations, And A Timely Setup Could Unlock Value Soon
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Park Hotels & Resorts (PK), I rated them a Buy, supported by their solid AFFO, conservative management, several portfolio adjustments, and a nearly double-digit dividend sustained by their AFFO.

Despite the stock returning over 15% since then, I will reiterate PK’s Buy rating following a solid report released recently, with the bull case supported by several internal and macro catalysts even in the near future, including the timing of their renovations, asset recycling, and important events that could help the travel industry.

Internal Developments

PK’s Q4 was solid overall, beating the market’s revenue and especially FFO estimates by quite a bit, with the adjusted FFO reaching $394 million in 2025, dropping from their $430 million reported in 2024, or about $1.97 per share compared to $2.06 as a result of ongoing overhauls, renovations, and asset sales, with the company noting that some of the non-core assets sold were operating at a weak 7% average EBITDA margin.

As for the guidance, they expect RevPAR growth between 0% and 2%, with Adjusted EBITDA reaching $580 million to $610 million and an Adjusted FFO per diluted share of $1.73 to $1.89, meaning a midpoint of $1.81, while expecting 201 million shares outstanding, getting us to a reported Adjusted FFO of $363.81

Note that the company’s Adjusted FFO is not what we normally call the AFFO (which is common in the industry), as the number they report doesn’t take into account the FF&E reserve (the CAPEX for Furniture, Fixtures, and Equipment), which is generally about 4% of hotel revenues, although some sources suggest that the current level in even double that much over the past decade, as the pandemic disrupted these numbers significantly as a result of a mix of inflation combined with occupancy drops, while others noted that many hotels haven’t been renovated in over a decade.

For these initiatives, the company also spent significant amounts on capital improvements (nearly $300 million), advancing on several renovations and expecting to spend about $230 million to $260 million in CAPEX in 2026, which could be accelerated by their recent non-core asset sales, basically trimming the non-performing segments of their portfolio to fortify their better performers.

S&P Global sees the fact that PK is willing to maintain buybacks at the expense of their leverage as a “material downside risk,” but it’s important to remember that every share they repurchase is a share less that gets the dividend, and thanks to today’s environment, they can basically save 8.75% (after the stock price went up in recent months) thanks to it, which is massive, likely being one of the best things the company can spend money on right now.

Financially, based on PK’s latest report, we can see an overall normal position for a REIT, with a decent amount of cash on hand and a lot of assets that cover their debt (weighted average interest rate of 5.15%), despite the value of the assets being depreciated.

As for the dividend, we are currently looking at a yield of ~8.75%, meaning roughly $200 million per year, which remains very well covered by the company’s reported Adjusted FFO and even the true AFFO, while also supporting their ~$45 million worth of buybacks, adding another ~2% to the yield.

Macro Developments

Since I last covered them, the overall environment didn’t improve, with US consumer confidence being reported at the lowest level since 2014, with rising levels of stress and worries due to lower income, higher costs of living, and shrinking budgets.

As mentioned before, this is normal in a high-rate environment given the tool’s purpose of slowing down the economy to fight inflation (which impacts discretionary budgets and borrowing costs), but this weakness has been accentuated by several factors that can affect the market’s perceived level of risk, having to take into account all the worries surrounding Japan (the largest foreign owner of US treasuries), new tariff fears not long ago, geopolitical pressure, rising levels of debt, crisis concerns, and several other issues, although recent data eased these fears a bit, allowing treasury yields to fall, yet they remain elevated.

We also have to keep in mind factors like the S&P Global report, which reaffirmed their BB rating (not terrible, to be honest) but revised the outlook to negative, reflecting their estimations of the company's leverage getting above 5.5x in 2026, which is not really uncommon for a REIT, but it's also not the best, and elevated levels of growth are not something you want to see in this environment, which is why I prefer peers like RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ).

As a REIT, PK can be even more impacted by a high-rate environment, which can lead to even worse levels of flexibility and more pressure on their operators, which the market likely takes into account already given the valuation and yield they’re traded at, which also limits their access to external capital, as issuing stock is basically out of the question given the yield, and issuing debt is also expensive.

However, the hotel industry can also benefit from a trend seen in almost every segment of real estate, with a drop in new construction as a result of the drop in occupancy combined with rising construction costs (higher costs of labor, materials, debt, etc.), with North America's hotel pipeline currently in construction dropping by 8.8% and the one in the final staging plan down 4.1% as of late 2025.

This is important, because even if there was a recovery in construction now, it would still take years until the finished properties could affect the market, potentially buying enough time for the current environment to improve and stabilize.

2026 could prove to be a solid year for the industry in general, as not only can it benefit from the normalization following the largest US Government shutdown in history back in 2025, but also an influx of tourism thanks to America’s 250th celebration or the FIFA World Cup, all of which are major events that can benefit PK’s operators.

However, if we’re looking strictly at travel data, things are still not that great, as recent US tourism data shows that even though global tourism spending increased by 6.7% in 2025, the US actually fell by 6% in terms of foreign visitors and 7% in international tourist spending, with uncertainty around politics and visas likely playing key roles (outside of budget issues) - although domestic travel continued to recover to a new record.

Recent data shows a potential improvement in demand for 2026 and 2027, while CoStar and Tourism Economics see a 1.7% increase in RevPAR during the World Cup months of June and July, and the timing could be great overall given PK’s renovations and potential macro improvements.

Risks Reiterated

Overall, the environment is likely going to remain pressured at least in the near-term, which can lead to more volatility and pressure on the company’s valuation, while the timing of their asset sales could result in value losses since this isn’t the best time to sell.

Meanwhile, there is also the risk that the environment can still deteriorate in a direction that not even the positive catalysts mentioned above would help, which is already risky given the company’s very high costs of external capital (especially on their equity).

Valuation

For PK’s DCF to Equity valuation, I’ll start with their AFFO guidance of $363.81 million and discount the FF&E reserve (assuming 5%) of $127 million (using $2.54 billion in revenue), getting us to $236.81 million in 2026 to capture their “true” AFFO potential in the transitory year they expect now, followed by a potential jump to $275 million in 2027, as the company could turn into a leaner, higher-quality business while also enjoying an improving environment.

This would be followed by a CAGR of 5% until year 5 (below the market’s estimates even for the revenue), given the potential macro improvements, followed by a 3.5% CAGR until year 10 and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a discount rate of 12.5%, which seems fair for a company like this in the current environment, using the 201 million shares outstanding they expect in 2026 (compared to 200 million in 2025).

This way, we get to an equity value of $2.88 billion, for an intrinsic value of $14.34 per share, which is above the current $11.42 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

I reiterate PK’s buy rating, as the valuation continues to be compelling, the high-yield dividend is well covered, and the timing of the company’s late 2026 and 2027 transition could be very rewarding.

At that point, they could meet a better environment with a leaner and stronger portfolio, with rooms that come back online at higher price points, offsetting the risks, in my opinion.

data.title: 24 Current Buys Fetched From Dogcatcher's 91 Top Dividend Dogs Over 10 Years
data.description:
Foreword

This article is based on 91 top Ten-Year collected dividend dogs as determined by the author's weekly selections of portfolio stocks over the past ten years

How The Dogcatcher Ranked the Companies

To build this list of 91 top Ten-Year collected dividend dogs, The Dividend Dogcatcher whittled the 520 potential companies, down to 120 by looking at the top monthly gainers over the past ten years. That survey also revealed the 40 top quarterly and 10 annual top dogs. He then used annual totals to rank each of 10 portfolios. Annual portfolio totals ranged from $2,342.55 (a 4.5% return) in 2023 to $11,966.62 (a 23% return) in 2021. the ten year median was $7155.10 (a 13.76% return).

Any collection of dividend stocks is more clearly understood when subjected to yield-based (dogcatcher) analysis. These 91 publicly traded Ten-Year collected dividend dogs are perfect for the dogcatcher process. Here, February 18 data focused on 75 still paying dividends. The full list of 91 is posted by descending dividend yield in the Afterword of this article.

Happily, 42 of 91 ordinary, and 24 of the 42 ‘Safer’ dividend-paying Ten-Year collected dividend dogs , live up to the Dogcatcher IDEAL of showing annual dividends from a $1K investment exceeding their single share prices.

As of 2/18/26, the 24 are: Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX), Credit Agricole (CRARF), EPR Properties (EPR), Aegon (AEG), George Risk Industries (RSKIA), Whitecap Resources (WCPRF), Imperial Brands (IMBBY), Columbia Banking (COLB), United Microelectronics (UMC), Donegal Group (DGICA), Bouygues SA (BOUYY), KeyCorp (KEY), Bar Harbor Bankshares (BHB), Magic Software (MGIC), Viatris (VTRS), MDU Resources (MDU), FNB Corp (FNB), Mowi ASA (MHGVY), MedicaL Facilities Corp (MFCSF), Secure Waste Infrastructure (SECYF), Extendicare (EXETF), Nokia (NOK), Leggett & Platt (LEG), and Armanino Foods (AMNF).

Many first-time investors regard the IDEAL of annual dividends from a $1K investment exceeding their single share prices as a buy signal or, at least, an invitation to look-closer. the “Safer” ranking is given to stocks whose free cash flow yield exceeds their dividend yield, Paying dividends from cash flow is safer than using borrowed money.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Analysts Estimated 19.72% To 110.60% Net Gains From Ten Top Ten-Year Dog Companies To February 2027

Six of ten top 2026 Ten-Year List Dogs by yield were among the top ten gainers for the current year based on analyst 1-year target prices. (They are tinted gray in the chart below). Thus, the yield-based forecast for these February Ten-Year top dogs was graded by Wall St. Wizards as 60% accurate.

Estimated dividends from $1000 invested in each of the highest-yielding stocks plus their aggregated one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, supplied the data points. Note: target prices from less than two analysts were not counted. Thus, ten probable profit-generating trades projected to February 18, 2027 were:

Dogcatcher 10 Top Ten-Year February Dogs By Net Gains

Opera Ltd (OPRA) was projected to net $1,106.01, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 23% greater than the market as a whole.

Superior Group of Companies (SGC) was projected to net $680.50 based on dividends, plus the median of target estimates from 3 brokers, less transaction fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 48% greater than the market as a whole.

Manhattan Bridge Capital (LOAN) was projected to net $669.46, based on the median of target price estimates from 25 analysts, plus annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 75% less than the market as a whole.

Mach Natural Resources (MNR) was projected to net $561.43 based on the median of target estimates from 7 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. A beta number was not available for MNR.

H&R Block (HRB) was projected to net $337.22 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 64% less than the market as a whole.

Sixth Street Specialty Lending was projected to net $264.80 based on the median of target estimates from 10 analysts, plus its last dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 30% under the market as a whole.

UMH Properties (UMH) was projected to net $240.79 based on the median of target prices from 8 analysts, plus annual dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% over the market as a whole.

Flowers Foods was projected to net $232.68 based on a median of target price estimates from 6 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 67% under the market as a whole.

Energy Transfer was projected to net $211.63, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 33% under the market as a whole.

Ennis (EBF) was projected to net $197.15 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 63% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 48.02% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these ten stocks. These gain estimates were subject to average risk/volatility 28% under the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

75 Dogcatcher Top Ten-Year Dogs Showed February Analyst Target Gains

75 Dogcatcher Top Ten-Year Dogs Dogs Showed February 1yr Total Return Gains

75 Dogcatcher Top Ten-Year Dogs Reveal February Yields

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top 10yr Dogs By Yield For February

February 2026 top 10Yr Dividend stocks represented five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Four financial services stocks occupied the first, third, fifth and sixth places: Prospect Capital (PSEC) [1]; Chimera Investment (CIM) [3]; Manhattan Bridge Capital (LOAN) [5]; Sixth street Specialty Lending [6].

Thereafter three energy stocks placed second, seventh, and eighth: Mach Natural Resources (MNR) [2]; SFL Corp (SFL) [7]; Energy Transfer (ET) [8].

One consumer staples (defensive) stocks occupied fourth place, Flowers Foods (FLO) [4]. A technology sector member placed ninth, Opera Ltd [9].

Finally, a lone industrials sector stock placed tenth, Sinotruk Hong Kong Ltd (SHKLY) [10], to complete the top ten February 2026 list of 2026 top 10Yr Dividend stocks by yield.

Top 10yr Dog Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 2/18/26-7

The above chart reveals 75 dividend-paying Dogcatcher Top Ten-Year Dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is displayed the cost per dividend-dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of these Dogcatcher Top Ten-Year Dogs is Prospect Capital at $5.63 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive is Western Digital (WDC) at $593.12 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Eighteen of Seventy-five Dividend Paying Top 10yr Dogs Show Negative Free-Cash-Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That means, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Eighteen dividend paying Dogcatcher Top Ten-Year Dogs stocks so-infected in February are Prospect Capital, Mach Natural Resources, Chimera Investment, Manhattan Bridge Capital, Suburban Propane Partners, Sunoco LP, Superior Group, Brookfield Renewable Partners, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners, Atea, Mazda Motors, Matthews International, Artesian Resources, Iron Mountain, First Horizon, ABN AMRO Bank, and ASE Technology. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, deals, tariffs, and artificial intelligence, too.

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Ten Top 10yr Dogs Back to "Fair-Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, nine in the top ten are already ideally priced. Beside those nine, already in the ideal zone, one more top-yield stock (SHKLY) is within $50 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that this company raises dividends or split shares. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 14.26% To 105.07% Top 10yr Dog Upsides To February 2027; (32) Three -6.52% to -19.84% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 27.43% Advantage For The 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Top 10yr Dogs As of February, 2027

Ten-top 10yr Dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten Top 10yr Dogs selected 2/18/26 by yield, represented six of eleven sectors. Energy, Health Care, Materials, Real Estate, and Technology missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend Top 10yr Dogs (34) To Deliver 45.28% Vs. (35) 35.53% Net Gains by All Ten Come February 18, 2027

Analyst 1-year targets indicated $5000 invested as $1,000 in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top tenTop 10yr Dogs delivered 27.43% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The fifth lowest-priced top-ten yielding stock, Opera Ltd showed top analyst-estimated gains of 110.60%.

The four lowest-priced top-yield Top 10yr Dogs for February were: Prospect Capital; Manhattan Bridge Capital; Flowers Foods; SFL Corp; Opera Ltd, with prices ranging from $3.04 to $12.24.

Five higher-priced Top 10yr Dogs as of February 18 were: Mach Natural Resources; Chimera Investment; Energy Transfer; Sixth Street Specialty Lending; Sinotruk, with prices ranging $13.44 to $146.57.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.
